basics.name: Marliette R. Matos, Ph.D.
basics.label: Functional Genomics Postdoctoral Associate, New York Genome Center
basics.summary: Human genetics and functional genomics scientist with expertise in large-scale genetic association analyses, multi-omic data integration, and machine learning approaches for regulatory variant interpretation. Experienced in leading cohort-scale research programs, building scalable computational workflows, and translating genomic findings into disease-relevant biological insights.

work.name: New York Genome Center
highlights: Expand machine learning approaches for functional variant prioritization through evaluation and integration of complementary predictive methods.
Build integrative analyses combining functional genomics and CRISPR perturbation data to improve variant-to-gene mapping and causal mechanism prioritization.

work.name: Albert Einstein College of Medicine
work.position: Human Genetics Researcher (PhD research; in collaboration with New York Genome Center, 2022–2026)
highlights: Led a cohort-scale human genetics study in primary CD4+ T cells, generating a pan-CD4+ T-cell multi-omic map to define regulatory mechanisms underlying immune phenotypes, age-associated variation, and disease-relevant biology.

Identified and prioritized thousands of regulatory variants influencing gene expression and chromatin accessibility through molecular QTL mapping, providing mechanistic context for disease-associated loci across 46 immune-mediated diseases and traits.
Trained and benchmarked sequence-based machine learning models of regulatory activity against empirical QTL data to predict effects of fine-mapped disease-associated variants in LD.
Developed scalable computational workflows for cohort-level multi-omic preprocessing, including normalization, quality control, and integrative downstream analysis.

work.name: Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai
work.position: Functional Genetics and Stem Cell Researcher
highlights: Investigated molecular and cellular effects of disease-associated genetic variants using stem-cell and gene-editing approaches, generating mechanistic insights into variant function in disease-relevant contexts.
Standardized experimental workflows, data collection, and analysis practices to reduce technical variability and improve reproducibility across studies.

volunteer.organization: Graduate Student Council, Albert Einstein College of Medicine
highlights: Partnered with faculty and administrators to represent student priorities and recommend curriculum improvements.

volunteer.organization: SACNAS Chapter, Albert Einstein College of Medicine
highlights: Organized professional development and outreach initiatives that strengthened engagement and supported representation in STEM.

volunteer.organization: Einstein Omics Club, Albert Einstein College of Medicine
highlights: Co-developed computational workshops and accessible training resources that lowered barriers to genomics data analysis.
